case_name: Browning v. Advocate Health & Hospital Corp.

summary.brief: Browning v. Advocate Health & Hospital Corp.. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: About two weeks after the gallbladder surgery, Joseph Browning had most of his bowel and part of his stomach removed and a bowel transplant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Joseph Browning was admitted to Advocate Lutheran General Hospital on February 10, 2015, after medical imaging scans revealed an inflamed gallbladder.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Surgeon Dr. Daniel Resnick, an Advocate employee, removed Browning’s gallbladder the following evening through a procedure called a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Browning began developing troubling symptoms the next day, including low blood pressure, atrial fibrillation, and abdominal pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On February 24, 11 days after removing Browning’s gallbladder, Dr. Resnick conducted exploratory surgery and determined that nearly all of the small bowel was ischemic and necrotic and needed removal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Brownings presented testimony from 12 liability witnesses, including 8 treating residents, and two expert witnesses regarding the standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court found the depositions fell under the hearsay exception in Illinois Rule of Evidence 801(d)(2)(D).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury awarded the Brownings $49.25 million in damages.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We agree with defendants on the inadmissibility of the discovery depositions under Rule 801(d)(2)(D), as the physicians were not Advocate’s agents when deposed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court denied defendants’ motion for a new trial.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: Abdominal pain and symptoms suggestive of sepsis
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Joseph Browning was admitted with an inflamed gallbladder and underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Postoperatively, he developed low blood pressure, atrial fibrillation, and abdominal pain, indicating a suspected sepsis infection.

requestables.name: CT Scan
requestables.description: CT scan of the abdomen to identify source of sepsis

reveal.result_summary: Dilation of small bowel, more fluid, possible mechanical bowel obstruction and perforation
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates potential bowel perforation and obstruction

requestables.name: Abdominal Fluid Analysis
requestables.description: Analysis of fluid removed from abdomen

reveal.result_summary: Fluid tested positive for two types of bacteria, indicating peritonitis

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with inflamed gallbladder and undergoes surgery

timeline_phases.name: Postoperative Complications
timeline_phases.description: Development of sepsis and subsequent management

timeline_phases.name: Critical Decision Point
timeline_phases.description: Decision to delay surgery despite worsening condition

timeline_phases.name: Exploratory Surgery
timeline_phases.description: Exploratory surgery reveals extensive bowel necrosis

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ICU with suspected sepsis post-cholecystectomy
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.clinical_reasoning: Timely surgical intervention could prevent further complications

options.description: Continue medical monitoring and delay surgery

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant delayed surgery, opting for continued monitoring

expected_action_court.description: Exploratory surgery should have been performed earlier
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated earlier surgery was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in surgical intervention led to worsened condition
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Exploratory surgery should have been conducted sooner

patient_outcome.description: Patient required removal of most of the bowel and part of the stomach, followed by a bowel transplant
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Loss of normal life, chronic health issues

key_findings: Failure to perform timely exploratory surgery
Causation established - delay led to irreversible bowel damage

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Earlier surgery could have prevented extensive bowel necrosis

factual_timeline.event: Patient admitted and underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy

tests_performed.result: Dilation of small bowel, possible obstruction and perforation

complications.complication: Bowel necrosis and need for transplant

allegations.allegation: Failure to perform timely exploratory surgery

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by delaying surgery
